entry.title: Combined summary - zkPoH: Zero-Knowledge Proof-of-Hodl
entry.summary: The recent advancement in Bitcoin privacy through zero-knowledge (zk) technologies introduces a new system called Zero-Knowledge Proof of Hodl (zkPoH). This method allows Bitcoin holders to prove control over a balance of at least 1 BTC without revealing sensitive transaction details such as the exact UTXOs, wallet addresses, or private keys. The zkPoH system utilizes an off-chain snapshot of Bitcoin UTXOs encoded into a Merkle tree, creating a public commitment via a Merkle root. During verification, a prover selects up to four UTXOs from this snapshot and uses Rust programming to generate witness inputs for a Noir circuit. This setup ensures that each chosen UTXO is part of the committed snapshot and that their cumulative value meets or exceeds 100 million satoshis. The prototype of this system incorporates Blake2s hashing for the Merkle trees and uses signed Wallet Import Format (WIF) proofs to verify ownership outside of the cryptographic circuit. Future enhancements may include integrating direct ownership verification within the circuit and supporting advanced Bitcoin protocols like Schnorr signatures and Taproot. Additional features under consideration involve snapshot epochs, nullifiers, Utreexo commitments, and customizable threshold values. While primarily educational at this stage, these developments mark significant progress towards enhancing privacy in Bitcoin transactions. For those interested, ongoing updates and contributions to this project can be tracked on GitHub at [github.com/fabohax/zkPoH](https://github.com/fabohax/zkPoH). Current security measures within zkPoH require a crucial ownership/signing step to ensure that the UTXOs used are indeed controlled by the prover. This involves the verifier issuing a challenge that the prover must sign using keys corresponding to the selected UTXOs. The proof then not only demonstrates the existence of UTXOs summing to the required threshold but also confirms that the prover controls the keys to these UTXOs. Two possible methods for verifying ownership include an off-circuit check, which could potentially compromise privacy by revealing UTXOs, and an in-circuit check that maintains strict privacy by verifying all elements internally within the circuit. The latter is considered superior for maintaining the privacy model. The discussion extends to potential applications such as the Lightning Network, where proving UTXO control could enhance privacy without disclosing specific funding outputs. This aligns with broader discussions among developers about leveraging zk technology to obscure channel funding sources while ensuring that UTXOs cannot be simultaneously used for multiple channels. These advancements suggest a future where Bitcoin transactions can achieve greater privacy and security, benefiting a wide range of users across various applications.
